refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005 Refined: The NGHE solution shall automatically derive performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) from worksite mission context and operational parameters, record them in a traceable baseline, and maintain a managed history of all requirement updates and calibration changes with full audit trail.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR was vague on 'method' and lacked explicit traceability and history management. Refinement adds specificity: (1) automatic derivation from mission context, (2) explicit baseline recording, (3) managed history with audit trail. This aligns with acceptance criteria demanding documented performance targets traceable to worksite inputs and maintained baselines/calibration updates. High safety criticality demands explicit safety margin derivation and audit capability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verification shall confirm: (a) performance requirements are automatically computed from worksite mission inputs (e.g., terrain, payload, cycle count); (b) baseline is recorded with timestamp, source, and derivation method; (c) history log captures all updates with reason, timestamp, and approver; (d) traceability chain from worksite input → derived requirement → baseline → history is complete and auditable; (e) safety margins are explicitly calculated and documented.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: Ingest worksite mission context (terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints) and operational parameters.
CR-NGHE-005.2: Compute performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) using mission-context-aware algorithms.
CR-NGHE-005.3: Record derived performance requirements as a baseline with metadata (timestamp, source, derivation method, approver).
CR-NGHE-005.4: Maintain a managed history of all performance requirement baselines and calibration updates with full audit trail (who, what, when, why).
CR-NGHE-005.5: Provide traceability links from worksite inputs → derived requirements → baseline records → history entries.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized Performance Derivation Engine – A dedicated software module that ingests worksite mission context via standardized APIs, applies mission-aware algorithms to compute performance targets, records baselines in a versioned database, and maintains an immutable audit log of all changes.

DP-B: Distributed Derivation with Local Caching – Performance derivation logic embedded in Vehicle and Teleoperation modules, with local caching of baselines and periodic synchronization to a central audit log.

DP-C: Manual Baseline Entry with Post-Hoc Validation – Site Manager manually enters performance targets via UI; system records entry and validates against mission context retroactively; history maintained in append-only log.

DP-D: Hybrid Centralized-Distributed – Core derivation engine (centralized) computes baselines; Vehicle and Teleoperation modules cache and apply locally; all changes flow back to central audit log for reconciliation.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Centralized Performance Derivation Engine – A dedicated software module that ingests worksite mission context via standardized APIs, applies mission-aware algorithms to compute performance targets, records baselines in a versioned database, and maintains an immutable audit log of all changes.

potential_sources: DP-A depends on standardized mission context input format (must couple with worksite input interface definition).
DP-A must couple with baseline database schema and audit log schema (design decisions must be coordinated with data architecture).
DP-A must couple with algorithm selection for performance computation (safety-critical algorithms require validation and certification).
coupling_check.notes: DP-A exhibits low coupling to Vehicle and Teleoperation operational logic; it is a service layer that consumes inputs and produces outputs. Coupling is primarily to data schemas and algorithm specifications, which are manageable design decisions. No circular dependencies detected. High safety criticality requires that algorithm derivation be decoupled from real-time operational control (good separation of concerns).

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite mission context is available in a structured, machine-readable format (e.g., JSON, protobuf) at system initialization and can be updated dynamically.
ASSUMPTION: Performance derivation algorithms are deterministic and can be validated/certified for safety-critical use.
ASSUMPTION: Baseline database and audit log can be implemented with sufficient performance and reliability for operational use.
RISK: If mission context format is ad-hoc or unstructured, derivation engine will require extensive parsing/validation logic, increasing complexity and defect risk.
RISK: If algorithms are heuristic or ML-based, certification and validation become significantly more difficult; recommend rule-based or physics-based algorithms.
RISK: If audit log becomes very large (high-frequency updates), query performance and storage may degrade; recommend partitioning and archival strategy.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Recommend DP-A with the following refinements: (1) Define a formal schema for worksite mission context input (e.g., XSD or JSON Schema) to ensure consistency. (2) Specify that performance derivation algorithms shall be rule-based or physics-based (not ML) to enable certification. (3) Define audit log retention and archival policy to manage storage and query performance. (4) Require that all baseline changes trigger a SafetyAlert notification to Safety Manager for high-criticality changes.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system integration boundary. It contains all subsystems and services, including the Performance Derivation Engine (a logical component within NGHE Solution). The Performance Derivation Engine shall be realized as a dedicated software service or library within NGHE Solution, with clear interfaces for mission context ingestion, baseline recording, and history management.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Worksite mission context (terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints, operational policies) flows into the Performance Derivation Engine via Tasking interface. This interface carries structured mission parameters that drive requirement derivation.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Derived performance baselines and requirement updates flow out via Telemetry interface to Vehicle and Teleoperation modules for operational use and monitoring. Telemetry also carries audit log entries and history records for external visibility.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: When performance requirements change significantly or safety margins are violated, SafetyAlert notifications are sent to Safety Manager and other stakeholders. This ensures high-criticality changes are visible and auditable.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Performance Derivation Engine may communicate with external systems (e.g., cloud-based analytics, regulatory databases) via Network Link to fetch mission context or validate requirements. Audit log may be synchronized to remote storage via Network Link.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system boundary and is the natural home for the Performance Derivation Engine. The engine is a core system service that supports all subsystems. Containment is very high because the engine is a logical component of the overall solution. Diffusion risk is minimal (5%) because the engine is a single, cohesive service with well-defined interfaces. No duplication or scattering across modules is expected.

Vehicle module is responsible for operational control and real-time execution. Placing the Performance Derivation Engine in Vehicle would couple requirement derivation to vehicle-specific logic, violating separation of concerns. High diffusion risk because derivation logic would need to be replicated or synchronized across multiple vehicle instances. Not recommended.

Teleoperation module is responsible for remote operator control and communication. Placing the Performance Derivation Engine in Teleoperation would couple requirement derivation to tele-operator workflows, which is inappropriate. Derivation should be independent of operational mode (autonomous vs. tele-operated). High diffusion risk. Not recommended.

Safety Services module is responsible for safety monitoring and enforcement. Performance requirements (especially safety margins) are closely related to safety. However, Safety Services should consume performance requirements, not generate them. Placing the derivation engine in Safety Services would create a circular dependency (Safety Services depends on performance requirements, but would also generate them). Moderate containment score reflects the safety relevance, but diffusion risk is high because derivation logic is not inherently a safety service. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Performance Derivation Engine is a system-level service that must be owned by NGHE Solution (the top-level integration boundary). It is not a subsystem-specific function; it is a cross-cutting concern that serves all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). Containment in NGHE Solution ensures: (1) single source of truth for performance requirements, (2) clear separation of derivation logic from operational logic, (3) centralized audit trail and history management, (4) minimal diffusion and duplication.
containment_risks: RISK: If NGHE Solution module is too large or poorly structured, the Performance Derivation Engine may become buried or difficult to locate. Mitigation: Define clear sub-module structure within NGHE Solution (e.g., NGHE Solution > Performance Management > Derivation Engine).

RISK: If interfaces between NGHE Solution and subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are not well-defined, the Performance Derivation Engine may become tightly coupled to subsystem implementations. Mitigation: Enforce strict interface contracts (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert) and use dependency injection or service locator patterns.

RISK: If the Performance Derivation Engine is not properly isolated from operational control logic, it may be inadvertently modified during vehicle operation, compromising audit trail integrity. Mitigation: Implement access control and versioning for the derivation engine; restrict modifications to designated configuration/maintenance windows.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Recommend assigning the Performance Derivation Engine to NGHE Solution with the following architectural refinements: (1) Define a clear sub-module structure: NGHE Solution > Performance Management Service > Derivation Engine, Baseline Repository, Audit Log. (2) Implement strict interface contracts for Tasking (input), Telemetry (output), SafetyAlert (notifications), and Network Link (external sync). (3) Use a service-oriented architecture pattern (e.g., microservice or library service) to ensure the engine is independently deployable and testable. (4) Implement role-based access control (RBAC) to restrict who can modify performance requirements and baselines. (5) Implement immutable audit logging with cryptographic signing to ensure audit trail integrity.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (Centralized Performance Derivation Engine) directly addresses all sub-FRs of CR-NGHE-005 Refined: (1) ingests mission context via APIs, (2) computes performance requirements using algorithms, (3) records baselines with metadata, (4) maintains managed history with audit trail, (5) provides traceability. DP-A is a logical, coherent solution that does not introduce unnecessary complexity or coupling. The selected DP is appropriate for the high safety criticality of the requirement.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK (Low): If the Performance Derivation Engine is not properly encapsulated within NGHE Solution, derivation logic may leak into Vehicle or Teleoperation modules. Mitigation: Enforce strict module boundaries and interface contracts.

DIFFUSION RISK (Low): If baseline database and audit log are not centralized, multiple copies may exist across modules, leading to inconsistency. Mitigation: Implement a single, centralized baseline repository and audit log service.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary for the Performance Derivation Engine. The engine is a system-level service, not a subsystem-specific function. Containment score of 95 reflects high confidence that NGHE Solution is the correct home. Diffusion score of 5 reflects minimal risk of scattering or duplication. The engine will interface with Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services via well-defined interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link), ensuring clean separation of concerns.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of CR-NGHE-005 Refined is feasible within NGHE Solution IM boundary. Verification approach: (1) Unit tests for derivation algorithms (given mission context, verify correct performance targets are computed). (2) Integration tests for baseline recording and history management (verify baselines are recorded with correct metadata and history is maintained). (3) Traceability tests (verify links from worksite inputs to derived requirements to baselines to history entries). (4) Audit trail tests (verify all changes are logged with who, what, when, why). (5) Safety margin tests (verify safety margins are correctly computed and documented). (6) Interface tests (verify Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link interfaces work correctly).
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: CR-NGHE-005 Refined is allocated to NGHE Solution > Performance Management Service > Derivation Engine. Sub-FRs are allocated as follows: CR-NGHE-005.1 (mission context ingestion) → Derivation Engine input interface (Tasking). CR-NGHE-005.2 (performance computation) → Derivation Engine core logic. CR-NGHE-005.3 (baseline recording) → Baseline Repository sub-module. CR-NGHE-005.4 (history management) → Audit Log sub-module. CR-NGHE-005.5 (traceability) → Traceability service (cross-cutting concern). Ownership is clear: NGHE Solution system engineer is responsible for the Performance Management Service.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1: Refine the Tasking interface specification to formally define the schema for worksite mission context (e.g., XSD, JSON Schema, protobuf). This will ensure consistent input to the derivation engine.

REVISION 2: Specify the performance derivation algorithms in detail (e.g., cycle time = f(terrain, payload, vehicle_capability), energy efficiency = f(cycle_time, power_consumption), availability = f(MTBF, MTTR)). Algorithms should be rule-based or physics-based, not ML-based, to enable certification.
REVISION 3: Define the baseline database schema and audit log schema explicitly. Specify retention policy, archival strategy, and query performance requirements.
REVISION 4: Specify the SafetyAlert notification protocol: which changes trigger alerts, who receives them, and what actions are required (e.g., approval, acknowledgment).
REVISION 5: Define the Network Link synchronization protocol for audit log and baseline repository (if external sync is required). Specify frequency, format, and error handling.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-005 Refined is not atomic; it has been decomposed into five sub-FRs (CR-NGHE-005.1 through CR-NGHE-005.5). Each sub-FR requires further refinement and design. The FR-DP-IM gates have passed, indicating that the current level is sound and ready for decomposition. Proceeding to the next level (ZIGZAG_DOWN) will allow detailed design of each sub-FR, including algorithm specification, database schema, interface contracts, and verification plans. High safety criticality demands thorough decomposition and detailed design.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: Ingest worksite mission context (terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints) and operational parameters.
CR-NGHE-005.2: Compute performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) using mission-context-aware algorithms.
CR-NGHE-005.3: Record derived performance requirements as a baseline with metadata (timestamp, source, derivation method, approver).
CR-NGHE-005.4: Maintain a managed history of all performance requirement baselines and calibration updates with full audit trail (who, what, when, why).
CR-NGHE-005.5: Provide traceability links from worksite inputs → derived requirements → baseline records → history entries.

assumptions: Worksite mission context is available in a structured, machine-readable format at system initialization and can be updated dynamically.
Performance derivation algorithms are deterministic and can be validated/certified for safety-critical use.
Baseline database and audit log can be implemented with sufficient performance and reliability for operational use.
NGHE Solution module structure supports a clear Performance Management Service sub-module with well-defined interfaces.
Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Network Link interfaces are available and can be extended to support performance requirement data flows.
Site Manager, Safety Manager, and Maintenance/Quality stakeholders have access to baseline and history records for audit and compliance purposes.
open_risks: RISK: If mission context format is ad-hoc or unstructured, derivation engine will require extensive parsing/validation logic, increasing complexity and defect risk. MITIGATION: Define formal schema for mission context input early in design.
RISK: If algorithms are heuristic or ML-based, certification and validation become significantly more difficult. MITIGATION: Restrict to rule-based or physics-based algorithms.
RISK: If audit log becomes very large, query performance and storage may degrade. MITIGATION: Define retention and archival policy; implement partitioning and indexing.

RISK: If Performance Derivation Engine is not properly encapsulated, derivation logic may leak into Vehicle or Teleoperation modules. MITIGATION: Enforce strict module boundaries and interface contracts.
RISK: If baseline database is not centralized, multiple copies may exist, leading to inconsistency. MITIGATION: Implement single, centralized baseline repository.
RISK: High safety criticality requires that algorithm derivation be certified and validated. MITIGATION: Plan for formal verification and certification activities in next level design.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of worksite mission context schema (XSD, JSON Schema, or protobuf).
Detailed specification of performance derivation algorithms (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) with mathematical formulas and validation criteria.
Database schema for baseline repository and audit log, including retention and archival policy.
Interface contract specifications for Tasking (input), Telemetry (output), SafetyAlert (notifications), and Network Link (external sync).
Role-based access control (RBAC) policy for baseline and history management.
Verification and validation plan for CR-NGHE-005 Refined, including unit tests, integration tests, traceability tests, audit trail tests, and safety margin tests.
Certification plan for performance derivation algorithms (if required by safety standards).
Stakeholder review and sign-off on performance requirement derivation process (Site Manager, Safety Manager, Maintenance/Quality).
